name: ज्ञानकर्मसंन्यासयोग
meaning: The Yoga of Knowledge and Action
teaching: Action done as offering stops binding the doer — it burns in the fire of knowledge like fuel. Krishna widens the lens: this teaching is ancient, the teacher returns age after age, and every sincere path of sacrifice ends in the same seeing. Knowledge is the rarest fire, and everything offered into it is purified. Knowledge here is not information but fire — it burns the bonds of past action and reveals stillness inside motion. The descent of the divine and the lineage of teachers exist to keep that fire findable.
questions: What, in your daily work, could you genuinely treat as an offering rather than a transaction?
Krishna says the wise see 'action in inaction, and inaction in action.' Sit with one day of your life through that lens.
What doubt, left uncut, is quietly governing your decisions?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
इमं विवस्वते योगं प्रोक्तवानहमव्ययम् ।
विवस्वान्मनवे प्राह मनुरिक्ष्वाकवेऽब्रवीत्॥
verses.transliteration:
imaṃ vivasvate yogaṃ proktavānahamavyayam
vivasvānmanave prāha manurikṣvākave'bravīt
verses.essence: I taught this imperishable yoga to Vivasvan; Vivasvan told it to Manu; Manu to Ikshvaku.
verses.translation: I taught this imperishable Yoga to Vivasvan; he told it to Manu; Manu proclaimed it to Ikshvaku.

anvaya.meaning: this yoga to Vivasvan (the sun)
I declared, imperishable
Vivasvan told it to Manu
Manu spoke it to Ikshvaku

verses.explanation: Krishna suddenly gives the teaching a genealogy older than history: sun, first man, first king. The claim is double — this yoga is not an improvisation for Arjuna's crisis but the original operating manual of civilization; and it travels by paramparā, person to person, like fire passed torch to torch.
verses.connection: Opens a new movement: the teaching of chapters 2–3 is now given its lineage and its license.
verses.reflection: Remember that what you are learning is ancient — let the lineage steady your private practice.
verses.background: Krishna abruptly widens the frame: this yoga he is teaching is ancient — spoken by Him to the sun-god Vivasvan at the dawn of things, handed down through royal sages, lost over long time, and now declared anew to Arjuna because he is His devotee and friend.

verses.devanagari:
एवं परम्पराप्राप्तमिमं राजर्षयो विदुः ।
स कालेनेह महता योगो नष्टः परन्तप॥
verses.transliteration:
evaṃ paramparāprāptamimaṃ rājarṣayo viduḥ
sa kāleneha mahatā yogo naṣṭaḥ parantapa
verses.essence: Thus received in succession, the royal sages knew it. Through long lapse of time, Parantapa, this yoga was lost to the world.
verses.translation: This, handed down thus in regular succession, the royal sages knew. This Yoga, by long lapse of time, has been lost here, O Parantapa (burner of the foes).

anvaya.meaning: thus received by succession
this the royal sages knew
it, by great time, here
this yoga was lost, O Parantapa

verses.explanation: Two details carry the weight: the keepers were rāja-ṛṣis, king-sages — rulers in the thick of action, not forest recluses; this yoga was always meant for people with kingdoms and battles. And even so, time ate it: truths are not lost by refutation but by generations of half-hearted transmission.
verses.connection: Explains why a restatement (4.3) is needed at all: the torch of 4.1 had gone out.
verses.reflection: Ask what great teaching has decayed in your keeping, and restore one small rite of it.
verses.background: Krishna abruptly widens the frame: this yoga he is teaching is ancient — spoken by Him to the sun-god Vivasvan at the dawn of things, handed down through royal sages, lost over long time, and now declared anew to Arjuna because he is His devotee and friend.

verses.devanagari:
स एवायं मया तेऽद्य योगः प्रोक्तः पुरातनः ।
भक्तोऽसि मे सखा चेति रहस्यं ह्येतदुत्तमम्॥
verses.transliteration:
sa evāyaṃ mayā te'dya yogaḥ proktaḥ purātanaḥ
bhakto'si me sakhā ceti rahasyaṃ hyetaduttamam
verses.essence: That same ancient yoga I have today declared to you, for you are my devotee and friend — this is the supreme secret.
verses.translation: That same ancient Yoga has been today taught to thee by Me, for thou art My devotee and My friend; it is the supreme secret.

anvaya.word: सः एव अयं मया ते अद्य
anvaya.meaning: that same (yoga) by me to you today
is declared, the ancient yoga

anvaya.word: भक्तः असि मे सखा च इति
anvaya.meaning: you are my devotee and friend
for this is the supreme secret

verses.explanation: The qualifications for the highest secret are not learning or austerity but two relationships: bhakta and sakhā, devotee and friend. Trust opens what intellect alone cannot — a secret is by definition something given, and giving requires a bond. Arjuna receives the lost yoga not because he is the best philosopher on the field, but because he is the one who loves and is loved.
verses.connection: Completes 4.1–2: the broken lineage is re-lit, and friendship is named as the conducting wire.
verses.reflection: Notice that the deepest things are entrusted to friendship — be the kind of friend secrets choose.
verses.background: Krishna abruptly widens the frame: this yoga he is teaching is ancient — spoken by Him to the sun-god Vivasvan at the dawn of things, handed down through royal sages, lost over long time, and now declared anew to Arjuna because he is His devotee and friend.

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
अपरं भवतो जन्म परं जन्म विवस्वतः ।
कथमेतद्विजानीयां त्वमादौ प्रोक्तवानिति॥
verses.transliteration:
aparaṃ bhavato janma paraṃ janma vivasvataḥ
kathametadvijānīyāṃ tvamādau proktavāniti
verses.essence: Arjuna asks: your birth was later, Vivasvan's earlier — how am I to understand that you taught it in the beginning?
verses.translation: Later on was Thy birth, and prior to it was the birth of Vivasvan (the Sun); how am I to understand that Thou taughtest this Yoga in the beginning?

anvaya.meaning: later is your birth
earlier the birth of Vivasvan
how am I to understand this
that you declared it in the beginning

verses.explanation: Arjuna does the arithmetic out loud: the sun is older than the man beside him in the chariot. The question is asked half in earnest, half as an opening — he suspects the answer will be larger than chronology, and he wants to hear Krishna say it. Good students build doorways with their doubts.
verses.connection: The natural objection to 4.1 — and the invitation Krishna needs to reveal who is actually speaking.
verses.reflection: Let yourself ask the naive question today; Arjuna's bluntness is why we have this book.
verses.background: Arjuna seizes the paradox with his characteristic bluntness: Vivasvan was born at the beginning; Krishna stands here, his own age. How could You have taught it first?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
बहूनि मे व्यतीतानि जन्मानि तव चार्जुन ।
तान्यहं वेद सर्वाणि न त्वं वेत्थ परन्तप॥
verses.transliteration:
bahūni me vyatītāni janmāni tava cārjuna
tānyahaṃ veda sarvāṇi na tvaṃ vettha parantapa
verses.essence: Many births of mine have passed, and of yours, Arjuna. I know them all; you do not, Parantapa.
verses.translation: Many births of Mine have passed as well as of thine, O Arjuna; I know them all but thou knowest not, O Parantapa (scorcher of foes).

anvaya.meaning: many of mine have passed
births, and of yours, Arjuna
them I know, all
you do not know, O Parantapa

verses.explanation: The answer begins with a leveling and a difference: both of them have worn many lives — rebirth is the shared condition — but only one of them remembers. The forgetting is the human predicament; the unbroken memory is the divine one. Krishna does not claim a different history from Arjuna's; he claims an unlost one.
verses.connection: Resolves 4.4's chronology: the body beside Arjuna is recent; the speaker is not.
verses.reflection: You too have passed through many selves you no longer remember — bow once to the forgotten ones.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
अजोऽपि सन्नव्ययात्मा भूतानामीश्वरोऽपि सन् ।
प्रकृतिं स्वामधिष्ठाय सम्भवाम्यात्ममायया॥
verses.transliteration:
ajo'pi sannavyayātmā bhūtānāmīśvaro'pi san
prakṛtiṃ svāmadhiṣṭhāya sambhavāmyātmamāyayā
verses.essence: Though unborn and imperishable, though Lord of all beings, ruling my own nature I come into being by my own maya.
verses.translation: Though I am unborn, of imperishable nature, and though I am the Lord of all beings, yet, governing My own Nature, I am born by My own Maya.

anvaya.meaning: though being unborn, of imperishable self
though being the Lord of beings
governing my own prakriti
I come into being by my own maya

verses.explanation: The paradox is stated, not softened: unborn, yet born. The resolution is in the instrumentals — adhiṣṭhāya, *governing* his nature rather than governed by it, and ātma-māyayā, by his own creative power rather than karma's compulsion. Ordinary birth is being thrown; this is stepping in. Same doorway, opposite direction of force.
verses.connection: Answers how 4.5's rememberer can have births at all: divine birth is sovereign, not sentenced.
verses.schools_note: 'Atma-mayaya sambhavami' — I take birth by My own maya: the schools divide on the avatara's body. Shankara: an appearance assumed by the ever-free Lord, real to us, binding to no one. Ramanuja: a true, non-material divine form taken by will, not karma. The Gaudiyas: the descent of an eternal form. All reject one reading — that the birth is karmic compulsion; 'sambhavami' is sovereign choice.
verses.reflection: The highest power arrives by choosing limits; honor one limitation of yours as a possible doorway.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत ।
अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānirbhavati bhārata
abhyutthānamadharmasya tadātmānaṃ sṛjāmyaham
verses.essence: Whenever dharma declines and adharma rises, Bharata, then I send myself forth.
verses.translation: Whenever there is decline of righteousness, O Arjuna, and rise of unrighteousness, then I manifest Myself.

anvaya.meaning: whenever indeed of dharma
there is decline, O Bharata
and the rising up of adharma
then I send forth myself

verses.explanation: The trigger condition for descent: glāni — dharma not destroyed but wilting, drooping like an unwatered plant — while its opposite stands up. The verse implies a stunning physics of history: the world's moral balance is monitored, and past a threshold, the Unborn enrolls in time. Yadā yadā — whenever — makes it a standing law, not a one-time rescue. Yada yada hi dharmasya — the avatara charter, recited at every festival of descent. The grammar is iterative: whenever, then — divine intervention as standing policy, not one-time event. Commentators count the classical descents but keep the clause open; Aurobindo read it inwardly as well: whenever dharma collapses in a single heart, the same descent is available there.
verses.connection: Generalizes 4.5–6: the principle behind every divine birth, including the one seated in Arjuna's chariot.
verses.schools_note: The avatara charter. Commentators count the classical ten descents under this verse, but split on scope: Shankara treats the principle as the Lord's recurring grace within the realm of appearance; Vaishnava schools as literal cosmic history with future installments pending. Aurobindo widened it inwardly: the same descent answers whenever dharma collapses in an individual heart.
verses.reflection: Watch for the dharma-decline in your own week — and for the help that rises exactly then.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
परित्राणाय साधूनां विनाशाय च दुष्कृताम् ।
धर्मसंस्थापनार्थाय सम्भवामि युगे युगे॥
verses.transliteration:
paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṃ vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām
dharmasaṃsthāpanārthāya sambhavāmi yuge yuge
verses.essence: For the protection of the good, the destruction of evil-doers, and the establishment of dharma, I come into being age after age.
verses.translation: For the protection of the good, for the destruction of the wicked and for the establishment of righteousness, I am born in every age.

anvaya.meaning: for the protection of the good
and for the destruction of evil-doers
for the firm establishment of dharma
I come into being, age after age

verses.explanation: The mission statement, in three clauses of widening scope: shelter the gentle, end the destroyers, and — the real objective — re-found dharma itself, since rescue without restoration only resets the clock. Yuge yuge promises recurrence: no age is abandoned. For Arjuna the implication is immediate and unspoken: this war is one such operation, and he is standing inside it. Sambhavami yuge yuge — I take birth age after age: the refrain of the verse most often set to music. The three purposes are read in order of depth — rescue of the good, dissolution of the harming, and dharma-samsthapana, the re-founding of the order itself. The schools differ on the body's nature (real, apparent, eternal-descended) but not on the promise's rhythm: again, and again.
verses.connection: Specifies the purpose of 4.7's descents — and silently reframes Kurukshetra as one of them.
verses.schools_note: The avatāra doctrine crystallizes here. Vaishnava schools read the descents as literal and fully divine; Advaita reads them as the formless appearing through maya for the world's sake — real to the world, dreamlike to the Absolute. Both agree on the function: dharma does not maintain itself.
verses.reflection: Protect one good thing today, discourage one corrosion, and you participate in the avatara's work.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
जन्म कर्म च मे दिव्यमेवं यो वेत्ति तत्त्वतः ।
त्यक्त्वा देहं पुनर्जन्म नैति मामेति सोऽर्जुन॥
verses.transliteration:
janma karma ca me divyamevaṃ yo vetti tattvataḥ
tyaktvā dehaṃ punarjanma naiti māmeti so'rjuna
verses.essence: One who truly knows my divine birth and action, on leaving the body is not born again — that one comes to me.
verses.translation: He who thus know, in their true light, My divine birth and action, having abandoned the body, is not born again, he comes to Me, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: my birth and action as divine
who knows thus, in truth
having left the body, rebirth

anvaya.word: न एति माम् एति सः अर्जुन
anvaya.meaning: does not attain — he comes to me, Arjuna

verses.explanation: An astonishing exchange rate: correct understanding of one fact — that this birth and these acts are divyam, divine and unforced — is itself liberating. Why so much from mere knowing? Because to truly see one being act without compulsion is to discover that action without bondage exists, and that discovery unlocks one's own. The knower of the free becomes free.
verses.connection: Attaches the fruit to 4.6–8: the doctrine of descent is not trivia — rightly grasped, it is a door.
verses.reflection: Hold the divine births as real once today, and notice what that does to your sense of company.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
वीतरागभयक्रोधा मन्मया मामुपाश्रिताः ।
बहवो ज्ञानतपसा पूता मद्भावमागताः॥
verses.transliteration:
vītarāgabhayakrodhā manmayā māmupāśritāḥ
bahavo jñānatapasā pūtā madbhāvamāgatāḥ
verses.essence: Freed from passion, fear and anger, absorbed in me, taking refuge in me, purified by the fire of knowledge, many have attained my being.
verses.translation: Freed from attachment, fear and anger, absorbed in Me, taking refuge in Me, purified by the fire of knowledge, many have attained to My Being.

anvaya.meaning: freed from passion, fear and anger
absorbed in me, taking refuge in me
many, by the austerity of knowledge
purified, have come to my being

verses.explanation: Precedent for the promise of 4.9: bahavaḥ, many — this road has traffic history. The sequence is exact: release the three contractions (craving, fear, anger), fill the cleared space with the Divine (man-mayāḥ — made of Me), and let jñāna work as tapas, knowledge itself as the purifying fire. The destination is not proximity but mad-bhāvam: his very mode of being.
verses.connection: Shows 4.9's door already worn smooth by use — and the inner steps of those who walked through.
verses.reflection: Take refuge from one fear today by knowledge of what you are, not by distraction.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
ये यथा मां प्रपद्यन्ते तांस्तथैव भजाम्यहम् ।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः॥
verses.transliteration:
ye yathā māṃ prapadyante tāṃstathaiva bhajāmyaham
mama vartmānuvartante manuṣyāḥ pārtha sarvaśaḥ
verses.essence: In whatever way people approach me, in that very way I receive them; all paths, Partha, are my paths.
verses.translation: In whatever way men approach Me even so do I reward them; My path do men tread in all ways, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: as people approach me
so indeed I receive them
my path they follow
human beings, O Partha, in every way

verses.explanation: One of the Gita's widest sentences. The divine response is a mirror with perfect resolution: approach as servant, find a master; as friend, a friend; as the formless, the formless; even as an enemy — the epics insist — and be met there too. And the second line dissolves the very idea of a wrong road: mama vartma... sarvaśaḥ — every path humans walk, knowingly or not, is already mine. Ye yatha mam prapadyante — as they approach me, so I receive them: the Gita's great verse of correspondence. Mama vartmanuvartante — all paths follow my road: the tradition reads here its deepest warrant for breadth — every sincere approach is answered in its own dialect — while the theistic schools add the note: answered by the same One, who is not generic.
verses.connection: Universalizes 4.10's 'many': the historical walkers were on countless roads, and all of them counted.
verses.schools_note: 'Ye yatha mam prapadyante' is the tradition's chief verse on religious breadth — and each school draws its line differently. Shankara: all worship reaches the one Self under whatever form. Ramanuja: the Lord reciprocates the exact mode of approach, but the approaches are not equal in fruit. Modern teachers (Ramakrishna, Gandhi) made it the charter of harmony of religions. The shared core: no sincere approach goes unanswered.
verses.reflection: However you approach the sacred today, trust that the approach is being answered in kind.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
काङ्क्षन्तः कर्मणां सिद्धिं यजन्त इह देवताः ।
क्षिप्रं हि मानुषे लोके सिद्धिर्भवति कर्मजा॥
verses.transliteration:
kāṅkṣantaḥ karmaṇāṃ siddhiṃ yajanta iha devatāḥ
kṣipraṃ hi mānuṣe loke siddhirbhavati karmajā
verses.essence: Desiring success in their works, people here sacrifice to the gods — for in the human world, success from action comes quickly.
verses.translation: Those who long for success in action in this world sacrifice to the gods; because success is ickly attained by men through action.

anvaya.meaning: desiring the success of actions
they sacrifice here to the deities
for quickly, in the human world
comes the success born of action

verses.explanation: A sociological aside without contempt: most religion is transactional because transactions pay fast. Rain, victory, children — the deities' department delivers on human timescales, while the path of 4.10 pays in a currency that takes lifetimes to recognize. The verse explains the market; it does not curse the customers.
verses.connection: The realistic counterpoint to 4.11: most approaches are quick-yield ones — and even those are received.
verses.reflection: Notice your preference for fast results — and what slower, truer thing it keeps outbidding.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
चातुर्वर्ण्यं मया सृष्टं गुणकर्मविभागशः ।
तस्य कर्तारमपि मां विद्ध्यकर्तारमव्ययम्॥
verses.transliteration:
cāturvarṇyaṃ mayā sṛṣṭaṃ guṇakarmavibhāgaśaḥ
tasya kartāramapi māṃ viddhyakartāramavyayam
verses.essence: The fourfold order was created by me according to division of guna and action. Though its maker, know me as the actionless, immutable non-doer.
verses.translation: The fourfold caste has been created by Me according to the differentiation of Guna and Karma; though I am the author thereof know Me as non-doer and immutable.

anvaya.meaning: the fourfold order was created by me
according to the division of guna and karma
though its maker, me
know as the non-doer, immutable

verses.explanation: Read the basis the verse itself gives: guṇa-karma-vibhāgaśaḥ — by temperament and work, not by birth; the verse describes a functional sorting of human natures, the kind every society performs. Its deeper cargo is the paradox in the second line: maker, yet non-doer — creation happens through his nature while he remains untouched, the template for the unattached action this whole chapter teaches.
verses.connection: Extends 4.6's sovereign action to world-making itself: even ordering society leaves no fingerprint on the Orderer.
verses.schools_note: 'Chatur-varnyam maya srishtam guna-karma-vibhagashah' — the four orders created by quality and work: the verse at the center of the caste debate. Classical commentators read guna-karma alongside birth; reformers from Vivekananda to Gandhi pressed the verse's own wording — temperament and deed, not parentage — as the Gita's actual criterion. The second half all schools cite: the Lord, though maker, is non-doer and unchanged.
verses.reflection: Read your own qualities and works honestly; let them, not labels, tell you your station.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
न मां कर्माणि लिम्पन्ति न मे कर्मफले स्पृहा ।
इति मां योऽभिजानाति कर्मभिर्न स बध्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
na māṃ karmāṇi limpanti na me karmaphale spṛhā
iti māṃ yo'bhijānāti karmabhirna sa badhyate
verses.essence: Actions do not stain me, nor have I craving for their fruit. One who knows me thus is not bound by actions.
verses.translation: Actions do not taint Me, nor have I a desire for the fruit of actions. He who knows Me thus is not bound by actions.

anvaya.meaning: actions do not stain me
nor in me is longing for action's fruit
who knows me thus
is not bound by actions

verses.explanation: The mechanism of divine immunity, stated so it can be borrowed: actions limpanti — smear, stain — only where craving for the fruit gives them adhesive. No spṛhā, no stickiness. And again the strange grace of 4.9: merely *knowing* this about Krishna transmits the immunity, because what is truly understood begins to be imitated from within.
verses.connection: Explains 4.13's maker-non-doer paradox — and offers the same loophole to whoever comprehends it.
verses.reflection: Work one hour today the way water moves over a lotus leaf — touching everything, stained by nothing.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
एवं ज्ञात्वा कृतं कर्म पूर्वैरपि मुमुक्षुभिः ।
कुरु कर्मैव तस्मात्त्वं पूर्वैः पूर्वतरं कृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
evaṃ jñātvā kṛtaṃ karma pūrvairapi mumukṣubhiḥ
kuru karmaiva tasmāttvaṃ pūrvaiḥ pūrvataraṃ kṛtam
verses.essence: Knowing this, the ancient seekers of liberation also performed action. Therefore do action, as the ancients did of old.
verses.translation: Having known this, the ancient seekers after freedom also performed action; therefore do thou also perform action, as did the ancients in days of yore.

anvaya.meaning: knowing thus, action was performed
even by the ancient seekers of freedom
therefore do action indeed, you
as done by the ancients of older time

verses.explanation: The clinching precedent: mumukṣubhiḥ — those whose one desire was liberation itself — did not quit the field; they worked, armored by the knowledge of 4.14. If the most freedom-hungry people in history chose action, action cannot be freedom's enemy. Tradition here is not a cage but a trail of footprints across difficult ground.
verses.connection: Closes the avatara section's practical loop: what the Lord does (4.13–14), the liberation-seekers copied — now Arjuna's turn.
verses.reflection: The ancients sought freedom and still acted; let their precedent end your either-or today.
verses.background: Krishna's answer unveils who is driving the chariot: many births of both of them, His remembered and Arjuna's not; the doctrine of the avatara — the unborn Lord taking birth by His own power whenever dharma declines; and His own example of action that leaves no stain.

verses.devanagari:
किं कर्म किमकर्मेति कवयोऽप्यत्र मोहिताः ।
तत्ते कर्म प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वा मोक्ष्यसेऽशुभात्॥
verses.transliteration:
kiṃ karma kimakarmeti kavayo'pyatra mohitāḥ
tatte karma pravakṣyāmi yajjñātvā mokṣyase'śubhāt
verses.essence: What is action? What is inaction? Even the wise are bewildered here. I shall teach you that action, knowing which you will be freed from evil.
verses.translation: What is action? What is inaction? As to this even the wise are confused. Therefore I shall teach thee such action (the nature of action and inaction) by knowing which thou shalt be liberated from the evil (of Samsara, the wheel of birth and death).

anvaya.meaning: what is action, what inaction
even the seers are bewildered here
that action I shall declare to you
knowing which you will be freed from evil

verses.explanation: A humbling admission opens the chapter's deepest passage: kavayo 'pi — even the poets-seers lose their way in this question. Because the answer is not behavioral: doing and not-doing cannot be told apart by watching the hands. Krishna promises a redefinition radical enough that even experts needed it.
verses.connection: Pivot to the chapter's core inquiry (4.16–23): the metaphysics of action that 4.13–15 presupposed.
verses.reflection: Admit that action and inaction puzzle even the wise — then welcome instruction over improvisation.
verses.background: The discourse turns to the koan even sages miss: action, inaction, and wrong action. Krishna teaches the seeing of stillness in motion, the burned seeds of consecrated work, contentment beyond acquisition — culminating in the brahmarpanam verse where act, offering, and offerer fuse into one.

verses.devanagari:
कर्मणो ह्यपि बोद्धव्यं बोद्धव्यं च विकर्मणः ।
अकर्मणश्च बोद्धव्यं गहना कर्मणो गतिः॥
verses.transliteration:
karmaṇo hyapi boddhavyaṃ boddhavyaṃ ca vikarmaṇaḥ
akarmaṇaśca boddhavyaṃ gahanā karmaṇo gatiḥ
verses.essence: For the nature of action must be understood, and of wrong action, and of inaction — profound is the way of action.
verses.translation: For verily (the true nature) of action (enjoined by the scriptures) should be known, also (that) of forbidden (or unlawful) action, and of inaction; hard to understand is the nature (path) of action.

anvaya.meaning: for of action there is much to be understood
and of wrong action to be understood
and of inaction to be understood
deep, hidden is the course of action

verses.explanation: Three categories, one warning: karma, vikarma (action gone wrong), akarma — and gahanā, the path is a thicket, dense and trackless. The triple repetition of boddhavyam, 'must be understood', hammers that this is knowledge-work, not rule-following. Morality by checklist fails exactly where it matters most.
verses.connection: Justifies 4.16's claim that experts get lost: the terrain itself is gahana, deep forest.
verses.reflection: Distinguish today between right action, wrong action, and the inaction that is also an act.
verses.background: The discourse turns to the koan even sages miss: action, inaction, and wrong action. Krishna teaches the seeing of stillness in motion, the burned seeds of consecrated work, contentment beyond acquisition — culminating in the brahmarpanam verse where act, offering, and offerer fuse into one.

verses.devanagari:
कर्मण्यकर्म यः पश्येदकर्मणि च कर्म यः ।
स बुद्धिमान्मनुष्येषु स युक्तः कृत्स्नकर्मकृत्॥
verses.transliteration:
karmaṇyakarma yaḥ paśyedakarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ
sa buddhimānmanuṣyeṣu sa yuktaḥ kṛtsnakarmakṛt
verses.essence: One who sees inaction in action and action in inaction is wise among humans — a yogi, a doer of all action.
verses.translation: He who seeth inaction in action and action in inaction, he is wise among men; he is a Yogi and performer of all actions.

anvaya.meaning: who sees inaction in action
and action in inaction
that one is wise among humans
that one is a yogi, doer of all action

verses.explanation: The famous double inversion. In action, inaction: the sage in full motion, with doership and craving absent, generates no karmic residue — the engine runs, nothing burns onto the self. In inaction, action: the abstainer of 3.6, body parked, mind seething, accrues karma while doing nothing. The ledger reads intent and identification, never the muscles. This single verse is the answer promised in 4.16. Karmany akarma yah pashyet — action in inaction, inaction in action: the Gita's most famous koan. Shankara reads it through the actionless Self appearing active; the karma-yoga tradition through stillness preserved at full engagement. Both honor the verse's claim: this seeing, not any doing or refraining, is what makes one buddhiman among humans.
verses.connection: The summit of 4.16–17: action and inaction redefined from the inside out.
verses.schools_note: The action-in-inaction riddle is each school's mirror: Shankara — the Self never acts, so the wise see stillness inside all motion and the deluded see a 'doer' even in sitting still; Ramanuja — true inaction is desireless action; the Karma-yoga tradition — engagement with inner silence. All read 'sa yuktah kritsna-karma-krit': this seeing, not retreat, completes all action.
verses.reflection: Find the stillness inside one busy task today, and the hidden current inside one quiet hour.
verses.background: The discourse turns to the koan even sages miss: action, inaction, and wrong action. Krishna teaches the seeing of stillness in motion, the burned seeds of consecrated work, contentment beyond acquisition — culminating in the brahmarpanam verse where act, offering, and offerer fuse into one.

verses.devanagari:
यस्य सर्वे समारम्भाः कामसङ्कल्पवर्जिताः ।
ज्ञानाग्निदग्धकर्माणं तमाहुः पण्डितं बुधाः॥
verses.transliteration:
yasya sarve samārambhāḥ kāmasaṅkalpavarjitāḥ
jñānāgnidagdhakarmāṇaṃ tamāhuḥ paṇḍitaṃ budhāḥ
verses.essence: One whose every undertaking is free of desire and scheme, whose actions are burned in the fire of knowledge — the wise call that one a sage.
verses.translation: He whose undertakings are all devoid of desires and (selfish) purposes and whose actions have been burnt by the fire of knowledge, him the wise call a sage.

anvaya.meaning: whose all undertakings
are devoid of desire and scheming will
whose actions are burnt by the fire of knowledge
him the wise call a pandit

verses.explanation: The portrait of 4.18's seer begins. Kāma-saṅkalpa is the two-stroke engine of bondage — the want, and the plotting that crystallizes around it; remove both and undertakings continue, oddly lighter. Jñānāgni — knowledge as fire — introduces the chapter's closing image: understanding does not merely inform action, it incinerates its binding residue on contact.
verses.connection: Begins translating 4.18's paradox into a recognizable human being (4.19–23).
verses.reflection: Let one undertaking today be free of desire's blueprint — and feel its karma burning clean.
verses.background: The discourse turns to the koan even sages miss: action, inaction, and wrong action. Krishna teaches the seeing of stillness in motion, the burned seeds of consecrated work, contentment beyond acquisition — culminating in the brahmarpanam verse where act, offering, and offerer fuse into one.

verses.devanagari:
त्यक्त्वा कर्मफलासङ्गं नित्यतृप्तो निराश्रयः ।
कर्मण्यभिप्रवृत्तोऽपि नैव किञ्चित्करोति सः॥
verses.transliteration:
tyaktvā karmaphalāsaṅgaṃ nityatṛpto nirāśrayaḥ
karmaṇyabhipravṛtto'pi naiva kiñcitkaroti saḥ
verses.essence: Abandoning attachment to the fruit of action, ever content, dependent on nothing — though engaged in action, such a one does nothing at all.
verses.translation: Having abandoned attachment to the fruits of the action, ever content, depending on nothing, he does not do anything though engaged in activity.

anvaya.meaning: abandoning attachment to action's fruit
ever content, dependent on nothing
though fully engaged in action
does nothing whatever

verses.explanation: The paradox stated at maximum voltage: abhipravṛttaḥ — plunged into activity — and yet 'does nothing at all.' The supports are two freedoms: nitya-tṛpta, contentment not awaiting the outcome, and nirāśraya, leaning on nothing external for standing. Doing without a doer hungry inside it is, karmically, a kind of perfect stillness in motion.
verses.connection: Restates 4.18's 'inaction in action' as lived posture: full throttle, zero grip.
verses.reflection: Begin something today without leaning on its outcome — content, independent, fully engaged.
verses.background: The discourse turns to the koan even sages miss: action, inaction, and wrong action. Krishna teaches the seeing of stillness in motion, the burned seeds of consecrated work, contentment beyond acquisition — culminating in the brahmarpanam verse where act, offering, and offerer fuse into one.

verses.devanagari:
निराशीर्यतचित्तात्मा त्यक्तसर्वपरिग्रहः ।
शारीरं केवलं कर्म कुर्वन्नाप्नोति किल्बिषम्॥
verses.transliteration:
nirāśīryatacittātmā tyaktasarvaparigrahaḥ
śārīraṃ kevalaṃ karma kurvannāpnoti kilbiṣam
verses.essence: Without longing, mind and self restrained, all possessions surrendered, performing action with the body alone — one incurs no fault.
verses.translation: Without hope and with the mind and the self controlled, having abandoned all covetousness, doing mere bodily action, he incurs no sin.

anvaya.meaning: free of longing, mind and self controlled
having abandoned all possessiveness
action merely bodily
performing, one incurs no sin

verses.explanation: The minimal case: action pared down to śārīraṁ kevalam — what the body must do to live and serve — with the three great accumulators (hope, mental sprawl, ownership) shut off. Such action passes through the person like light through glass. This is the floor of blameless living; the ceiling was 4.20's full engagement.
verses.connection: Gives 4.20 its ascetic register: even at survival-level action, the same principle holds.
verses.reflection: Do one bodily task today with hope and ownership both set down — and notice no residue forms.
verses.background: The discourse turns to the koan even sages miss: action, inaction, and wrong action. Krishna teaches the seeing of stillness in motion, the burned seeds of consecrated work, contentment beyond acquisition — culminating in the brahmarpanam verse where act, offering, and offerer fuse into one.

verses.devanagari:
यदृच्छालाभसन्तुष्टो द्वन्द्वातीतो विमत्सरः ।
समः सिद्धावसिद्धौ च कृत्वापि न निबध्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
yadṛcchālābhasantuṣṭo dvandvātīto vimatsaraḥ
samaḥ siddhāvasiddhau ca kṛtvāpi na nibadhyate
verses.essence: Content with whatever comes unsought, beyond the pairs, free of envy, equal in success and failure — even acting, one is not bound.
verses.translation: Content with what comes to him without effort, free from the pairs of opposites and envy, even-minded in success and failure, though acting, he is not bound.

anvaya.meaning: content with gain arriving of itself
beyond the pairs of opposites, free of envy
equal in success and in failure
even having acted, is not bound

verses.explanation: Four signatures of the unbound worker, each a closed leak: takes what the day brings (yadṛcchā-lābha) instead of campaigning for more; stands outside the hot-cold, praise-blame seesaw; vimatsara — does not measure himself against others' plates; and meets siddhi and asiddhi with the same face. Where nothing is leaking, nothing binds.
verses.connection: Completes the portrait of 4.19–21 with its social and emotional weather — equanimity as the seal.
verses.reflection: Be satisfied with what arrives unsought today, just once, and taste the rare flavor of enough.
verses.background: The discourse turns to the koan even sages miss: action, inaction, and wrong action. Krishna teaches the seeing of stillness in motion, the burned seeds of consecrated work, contentment beyond acquisition — culminating in the brahmarpanam verse where act, offering, and offerer fuse into one.

verses.devanagari:
गतसङ्गस्य मुक्तस्य ज्ञानावस्थितचेतसः ।
यज्ञायाचरतः कर्म समग्रं प्रविलीयते॥
verses.transliteration:
gatasaṅgasya muktasya jñānāvasthitacetasaḥ
yajñāyācarataḥ karma samagraṃ pravilīyate
verses.essence: For one unattached and free, mind established in knowledge, acting for sacrifice — all karma dissolves entirely.
verses.translation: To one who is devoid of attchment, who is liberated, whose mind is established in knowledge, who works for the sake of sacrifice (for the sake of God), the whole action is dissolved.

anvaya.meaning: of one whose attachment is gone, who is free
whose mind is established in knowledge
acting for the sake of yajna
(his karma) dissolves entirely

verses.explanation: The section's closing chord gathers its three notes: attachment gone, mind seated in knowledge, and the act offered as yajña — chapter 3's word returning, about to be vastly enlarged. Pravilīyate: the karma does not get balanced or repaid; it melts, like writing in water. Action can be made completely traceless.
verses.connection: Seals 4.18–22 and hands the torch to the yajna-expansion of 4.24–33.
verses.reflection: Work today as worship and watch the action dissolve like the offering it has become.
verses.background: The discourse turns to the koan even sages miss: action, inaction, and wrong action. Krishna teaches the seeing of stillness in motion, the burned seeds of consecrated work, contentment beyond acquisition — culminating in the brahmarpanam verse where act, offering, and offerer fuse into one.

verses.devanagari:
ब्रह्मार्पणं ब्रह्म हविर्ब्रह्माग्नौ ब्रह्मणा हुतम् ।
ब्रह्मैव तेन गन्तव्यं ब्रह्मकर्मसमाधिना॥
verses.transliteration:
brahmārpaṇaṃ brahma havirbrahmāgnau brahmaṇā hutam
brahmaiva tena gantavyaṃ brahmakarmasamādhinā
verses.essence: Brahman is the offering, Brahman the oblation, poured by Brahman into the fire of Brahman; Brahman is reached by one who sees Brahman in every act.
verses.translation: Brahman is the oblation; Brahman is the melted butter (ghee); by Brahman is the oblation poured into the fire of Brahman; Brahman verily shall be reached by him who always sees Brahman in action.

anvaya.meaning: Brahman is the ladle, Brahman the oblation
into the fire of Brahman, by Brahman offered
Brahman alone is to be reached by him
whose samadhi is in Brahman-as-action

verses.explanation: The sacrifice analyzed into its five components — instrument, offering, fire, offerer, act — and each found to be the same one substance. When every coordinate of an action is seen as Brahman, the action stops being a transaction between separate things and becomes the Absolute moving within itself. This verse is recited before meals across India: the most ordinary act of eating, lifted whole into the vision. Brahmarpanam brahma havih — the verse chanted before meals across the Hindu world, turning every table into the altar it describes. Commentators call it the summit of karma-yoga's logic: when offering, offered, fire, and offerer are seen as one Brahman, the act has nowhere to land as karma. Eating becomes the daily rehearsal of nonduality.
verses.connection: Detonates the word yajna from 4.23: sacrifice is no longer a rite within reality — reality itself is the rite.
verses.schools_note: Advaita's most cherished Gita verse: Shankara reads it as the knowledge that dissolves karma — non-difference of act, actor and goal. Ramanuja keeps the elements distinct but all ensouled by Brahman. Either way, it is the metaphysical engine behind 'action that binds nothing.'
verses.reflection: Before one meal or task, see Brahman as the offering, the act, the doer — and the day turns altar.
verses.background: The discourse turns to the koan even sages miss: action, inaction, and wrong action. Krishna teaches the seeing of stillness in motion, the burned seeds of consecrated work, contentment beyond acquisition — culminating in the brahmarpanam verse where act, offering, and offerer fuse into one.

verses.devanagari:
दैवमेवापरे यज्ञं योगिनः पर्युपासते ।
ब्रह्माग्नावपरे यज्ञं यज्ञेनैवोपजुह्वति॥
verses.transliteration:
daivamevāpare yajñaṃ yoginaḥ paryupāsate
brahmāgnāvapare yajñaṃ yajñenaivopajuhvati
verses.essence: Some yogis offer sacrifice to the gods; others offer sacrifice itself into the fire of Brahman.
verses.translation: Some Yogies perform sacrifice to the gods alone; while others (who have realised the Self) offer the self as sacrifice by the Self in the fire of Brahman alone.

anvaya.meaning: to the gods alone, some, sacrifice
yogis perform and attend
into the fire of Brahman, others, sacrifice
by sacrifice itself offer up

verses.explanation: A catalogue of fires begins (4.25–30), and its first entry sets the range: from the familiar deva-yajña of altar and ghee to the vertiginous act of offering the very sense of separate sacrifice into the One. Every spiritual practice, the list will argue, is some arrangement of offering and fire.
verses.connection: Begins cashing out 4.24: if all is Brahman, then everything humans do toward the sacred is a species of one yajna.
verses.reflection: Whatever your form of sacrifice — service, study, craft — perform it today as sacrifice, knowingly.
verses.background: Krishna catalogues the many fires kept by seekers — offerings of senses, possessions, austerity, breath, study — democratizing the altar across every temperament, and ranks the sacrifice of knowledge above all offerings of substance, for all action culminates in knowing.

verses.devanagari:
श्रोत्रादीनीन्द्रियाण्यन्ये संयमाग्निषु जुह्वति ।
शब्दादीन्विषयानन्य इन्द्रियाग्निषु जुह्वति॥
verses.transliteration:
śrotrādīnīndriyāṇyanye saṃyamāgniṣu juhvati
śabdādīnviṣayānanya indriyāgniṣu juhvati
verses.essence: Some offer hearing and the other senses into the fires of restraint; others offer sound and the other objects into the fires of the senses.
verses.translation: Some again offer the organ of hearing and other senses as sacrifice in the fire of restraint; others offer sound and other objects of the senses as sacrifice in the fire of the senses.

anvaya.meaning: hearing and the other senses
others offer into the fires of restraint
sound and the other objects, others
offer into the fires of the senses

verses.explanation: Two opposite disciplines, both consecrated: the ascetic pours the senses themselves into restraint's fire — withdrawal as oblation; the householder pours objects into the senses' fire — engagement, but as conscious offering rather than grabbing. The Gita refuses to rank them; what sanctifies is the offering-posture, not the direction of flow.
verses.connection: Extends 4.25 inward: renunciation and right enjoyment are both fires, both valid altars.
verses.reflection: Offer one sense into restraint today, or one object into a sense, deliberately — both are valid fires.
verses.background: Krishna catalogues the many fires kept by seekers — offerings of senses, possessions, austerity, breath, study — democratizing the altar across every temperament, and ranks the sacrifice of knowledge above all offerings of substance, for all action culminates in knowing.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वाणीन्द्रियकर्माणि प्राणकर्माणि चापरे ।
आत्मसंयमयोगाग्नौ जुह्वति ज्ञानदीपिते॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvāṇīndriyakarmāṇi prāṇakarmāṇi cāpare
ātmasaṃyamayogāgnau juhvati jñānadīpite
verses.essence: Others offer all the workings of the senses and of the life-breath into the fire of self-restraint kindled by knowledge.
verses.translation: Others again sacrifice all the functions of the senses and those of the breath (vital energy or Prana) in the fire of the Yoga of self-restraint kindled by knowledge.

anvaya.meaning: all the actions of the senses
and the actions of the life-breath, others
into the fire of the yoga of self-restraint
offer, kindled by knowledge

verses.explanation: The totalizing version: not this sense or that object, but the entire traffic of perception and vitality, fed into one inward fire — and the kindling is named: jñāna-dīpite, lit by knowledge. Discipline without understanding is cold ash; understanding makes restraint combust into transformation.
verses.connection: Deepens 4.26: from selective offerings to the whole apparatus, with knowledge as the match.
verses.reflection: Pour a scattered hour into one-pointed attention and call it by its true name: oblation.
verses.background: Krishna catalogues the many fires kept by seekers — offerings of senses, possessions, austerity, breath, study — democratizing the altar across every temperament, and ranks the sacrifice of knowledge above all offerings of substance, for all action culminates in knowing.

verses.devanagari:
द्रव्ययज्ञास्तपोयज्ञा योगयज्ञास्तथापरे ।
स्वाध्यायज्ञानयज्ञाश्च यतयः संशितव्रताः॥
verses.transliteration:
dravyayajñāstapoyajñā yogayajñāstathāpare
svādhyāyajñānayajñāśca yatayaḥ saṃśitavratāḥ
verses.essence: Others offer wealth, austerity and yoga as sacrifice; ascetics of sharp vows offer study and knowledge.
verses.translation: Others again offer wealth, austerity and Yoga as sacrifice, while the ascetics of self-restraint and rigid vows offer study of scriptures and knowledge as sacrifice.

anvaya.meaning: sacrificers of wealth, sacrificers of austerity
sacrificers of yoga, likewise others
and sacrificers of study and of knowledge
ascetics of sharpened vows

verses.explanation: The list widens to cover whole lifestyles: the philanthropist's giving, the ascetic's heat, the yogi's practice, the scholar's svādhyāya — each a complete religious path when performed as offering. The Gita is quietly dissolving the monopoly of the ritual priesthood: anything poured out with the right inner posture is Vedic fire.
verses.connection: Continues 4.25–27's democratization of yajna into the great human vocations.
verses.reflection: Choose your altar today — possessions, austerity, practice, or study — and place something real on it.
verses.background: Krishna catalogues the many fires kept by seekers — offerings of senses, possessions, austerity, breath, study — democratizing the altar across every temperament, and ranks the sacrifice of knowledge above all offerings of substance, for all action culminates in knowing.

verses.devanagari:
अपाने जुह्वति प्राणं प्राणेऽपानं तथापरे ।
प्राणापानगती रुद्ध्वा प्राणायामपरायणाः॥
verses.transliteration:
apāne juhvati prāṇaṃ prāṇe'pānaṃ tathāpare
prāṇāpānagatī ruddhvā prāṇāyāmaparāyaṇāḥ
verses.essence: Others offer the inbreath into the outbreath and the outbreath into the inbreath, restraining both — intent on pranayama.
verses.translation: Others offer as sacrifice the outgoing breath in the incoming, and the incoming in the outgoing, restraining the course of the outgoing and the incoming breaths, solely absorbed in the restraint of the breath.

anvaya.meaning: into the apana (some) offer the prana
into the prana the apana, likewise others
restraining the courses of prana and apana
wholly intent on pranayama

verses.explanation: Breath itself becomes the altar: outflow offered into inflow, inflow into outflow, and in the third practice both held — the moment of stillness between breaths treated as the sanctum. The body's most automatic act, made deliberate, becomes liturgy. Pranayama enters the Gita not as exercise but as sacrifice.
verses.connection: The subtlest fire yet in the catalogue: the offering and the fire are both made of breath.
verses.reflection: Watch your breath for ten cycles, in and out as mutual offerings, and find the oldest ritual.
verses.background: Krishna catalogues the many fires kept by seekers — offerings of senses, possessions, austerity, breath, study — democratizing the altar across every temperament, and ranks the sacrifice of knowledge above all offerings of substance, for all action culminates in knowing.

verses.devanagari:
अपरे नियताहाराः प्राणान्प्राणेषु जुह्वति ।
सर्वेऽप्येते यज्ञविदो यज्ञक्षपितकल्मषाः॥
verses.transliteration:
apare niyatāhārāḥ prāṇānprāṇeṣu juhvati
sarve'pyete yajñavido yajñakṣapitakalmaṣāḥ
verses.essence: Others, of regulated food, offer the breaths into the breaths. All these are knowers of sacrifice, their stains destroyed by sacrifice.
verses.translation: Others who regulate their diet offer life-breaths in life-breaths. All these are knowers of sacrifice, whose sins are destroyed by sacrifice.

anvaya.meaning: others, of regulated food
offer the vital airs into the vital airs
all these indeed are knowers of yajna
their impurities destroyed by yajna

verses.explanation: The catalogue closes with even fasting included — life-force offered into life-force — and then the great equalizing verdict: sarve 'pi ete, *all* of these, from the ghee-pourer to the breath-watcher, are yajña-vids whose stains the practice burns. No fire on the list outranks another; the flame is the same flame.
verses.connection: Concludes the survey of 4.25–30 and certifies every entry: the form varies, the purification does not.
verses.reflection: Even your disciplined eating is a sacrifice; treat one meal today with a priest's attention.
verses.background: Krishna catalogues the many fires kept by seekers — offerings of senses, possessions, austerity, breath, study — democratizing the altar across every temperament, and ranks the sacrifice of knowledge above all offerings of substance, for all action culminates in knowing.

verses.devanagari:
यज्ञशिष्टामृतभुजो यान्ति ब्रह्म सनातनम् ।
नायं लोकोऽस्त्ययज्ञस्य कुतोऽन्यः कुरुसत्तम॥
verses.transliteration:
yajñaśiṣṭāmṛtabhujo yānti brahma sanātanam
nāyaṃ loko'styayajñasya kuto'nyaḥ kurusattama
verses.essence: Eaters of the nectar of sacrifice's remnant go to the eternal Brahman. Not even this world is for the non-sacrificer — how then the other, Kurusattama?
verses.translation: Those who eat the remnants of the sacrifice, which are like nectar, go to the eternal Brahman. This world is not for the man who does not perform sacrifice; how then can he have the other, O Arjuna?

anvaya.meaning: eaters of the nectar of yajna's remnant
go to the eternal Brahman
this world is not for the non-sacrificer
how then the other, O best of Kurus

verses.explanation: Chapter 3's leftover-eaters (3.13) return transfigured: the remnant of any true offering is now amṛta, nectar, and its eaters travel to Brahman itself. The closing logic cuts hard: the non-offerer fails even here — a life of pure taking cannot build a livable world, let alone a higher one. Sacrifice is not piety added to life; it is life's load-bearing wall.
verses.connection: Caps the catalogue with destination and warning, echoing 3.12–13 at a higher octave.
verses.reflection: Taste the remnant of sacrifice — the peace after selfless work — and let it spoil cheaper sweets.
verses.background: Krishna catalogues the many fires kept by seekers — offerings of senses, possessions, austerity, breath, study — democratizing the altar across every temperament, and ranks the sacrifice of knowledge above all offerings of substance, for all action culminates in knowing.

verses.devanagari:
एवं बहुविधा यज्ञा वितता ब्रह्मणो मुखे ।
कर्मजान्विद्धि तान्सर्वानेवं ज्ञात्वा विमोक्ष्यसे॥
verses.transliteration:
evaṃ bahuvidhā yajñā vitatā brahmaṇo mukhe
karmajānviddhi tānsarvānevaṃ jñātvā vimokṣyase
verses.essence: Thus many kinds of sacrifice are spread in the mouth of Brahman. Know them all as born of action; knowing thus, you shall be freed.
verses.translation: Thus, manifold sacrifices are spread out before Brahman (literally) at the mouth or face of Brahman). Know them all as born of action and thus knowing, thou shalt be liberated.

anvaya.meaning: thus manifold sacrifices
are spread out in the mouth of Brahman (the Veda)
know them all as born of action
knowing thus, you shall be released

verses.explanation: Two liberating observations: the many fires all open into one mouth — diversity of practice, single destination; and karma-jān, every one of them is made of *action*, of body, speech and breath actually doing something. No sacrifice on the list is performed by the Self, which only watches. See that, and the doer-knot loosens for good.
verses.connection: Distills 4.25–31: all the fires are one fire, and all of them burn on the far side of the witnessing Self.
verses.reflection: Many fires, one altar: stop ranking your neighbor's discipline against yours today.
verses.background: Krishna catalogues the many fires kept by seekers — offerings of senses, possessions, austerity, breath, study — democratizing the altar across every temperament, and ranks the sacrifice of knowledge above all offerings of substance, for all action culminates in knowing.

verses.devanagari:
श्रेयान्द्रव्यमयाद्यज्ञाज्ज्ञानयज्ञः परन्तप ।
सर्वं कर्माखिलं पार्थ ज्ञाने परिसमाप्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
śreyāndravyamayādyajñājjñānayajñaḥ parantapa
sarvaṃ karmākhilaṃ pārtha jñāne parisamāpyate
verses.essence: Better than the sacrifice of things is the sacrifice of knowledge, Parantapa; all action without remainder, Partha, culminates in knowledge.
verses.translation: Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

anvaya.meaning: better than the sacrifice of material things
is the sacrifice of knowledge, O Parantapa
all action, without remainder, O Partha
reaches its culmination in knowledge

verses.explanation: After honoring every fire equally, one ranking after all: jñāna-yajña stands above the offerings of stuff — not because things are base, but because all action, traced to its end, parisamāpyate, terminates in knowing. Works are the river; knowledge is the sea they were always running toward. The chapter now turns to how such knowledge is obtained.
verses.connection: The hinge from the yajna-catalogue to the knowledge-finale (4.34–42): every fire was kindling for this one.
verses.reflection: Prefer the knowledge-offering today: understand one thing deeply rather than performing three things outwardly.
verses.background: Krishna catalogues the many fires kept by seekers — offerings of senses, possessions, austerity, breath, study — democratizing the altar across every temperament, and ranks the sacrifice of knowledge above all offerings of substance, for all action culminates in knowing.

verses.devanagari:
तद्विद्धि प्रणिपातेन परिप्रश्नेन सेवया ।
उपदेक्ष्यन्ति ते ज्ञानं ज्ञानिनस्तत्त्वदर्शिनः॥
verses.transliteration:
tadviddhi praṇipātena paripraśnena sevayā
upadekṣyanti te jñānaṃ jñāninastattvadarśinaḥ
verses.essence: Learn that by prostration, by inquiry, by service; the knowers, the seers of truth, will teach you knowledge.
verses.translation: Know That by long prostration, by estion and by service; the wise who have realised the Truth will instruct thee in (that) knowledge.

anvaya.meaning: know that by prostration
by repeated inquiry, by service
they will instruct you in knowledge
the knowers, the seers of truth

verses.explanation: How the highest yajña is lit: not from books alone but from the lit. Three approaches form the protocol — praṇipāta, the bow that lowers the vessel below the source; paripraśna, questioning that is persistent yet sincere (not the cavilling of 3.32); sevā, service that proves the asking is serious. And the teacher must be doubly qualified: jñānin *and* tattva-darśin — learned, and having seen. Tad viddhi pranipatena — the verse that founded the guru-disciple protocol: prostration, inquiry, service — the three keys in order. Commentators stress pariprashnena, the questioning: this tradition does not ask for blind reception; it asks for engaged humility. And the teachers are tattva-darshinah — those who have seen, not merely read. Transmission, here, is from sight to sight.
verses.connection: Answers the question 4.33 raises: where does knowledge-sacrifice get its flame? From those already burning.
verses.schools_note: The guru-verse. Tradition derives the protocol from its three instrumentals: pranipata (approach with humility), pariprashna (question thoroughly — Shankara stresses inquiry is commanded, not discouraged), seva (serve, which opens the transmission). 'Tattva-darshinah' sets the bar each school keeps: the teacher must have seen, not merely studied; lineage exists to conserve sight.
verses.reflection: Approach someone who knows, with a real question, real respect, and real willingness to serve.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the way to that knowledge and a call to arms: approach the seers with prostration, inquiry, and service; knowledge as the raft that carries even the worst sinner, the fire that burns karma, the purifier found in oneself by time — then, doubt slain by the sword of knowledge, arise.

verses.devanagari:
यज्ज्ञात्वा न पुनर्मोहमेवं यास्यसि पाण्डव ।
येन भूतान्यशेषेण द्रक्ष्यस्यात्मन्यथो मयि (var अशेषाणि)॥
verses.transliteration:
yajjñātvā na punarmohamevaṃ yāsyasi pāṇḍava
yena bhūtānyaśeṣāṇi drakṣyasyātmanyatho mayi
verses.essence: Knowing which, you will not fall into delusion again, Pandava — by it you will see all beings in the Self, and then in me.
verses.translation: Knowing ï1thatï1 thou shalt not, O Arjuna, again get deluded like this; and by that thou shalt see all beings in thy Self and also in Me.

anvaya.meaning: knowing which, not again to delusion
thus will you go, O Pandava
by which, beings without remainder
you will see in the Self, and then in me

verses.explanation: What the teachers of 4.34 transmit, in two movements: first, all beings seen within the one Self — separateness healed; then, that Self seen within Krishna — the impersonal vision completing itself in the personal. And the practical guarantee Arjuna most needs: na punar moham, no relapse. The confusion of chapter 1 is not to be managed but ended.
verses.connection: States the content and the cure: this knowledge answers 1.28–47 at the root, permanently.
verses.reflection: Imagine seeing all beings in yourself for one minute — and let delusion lose its favorite argument.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the way to that knowledge and a call to arms: approach the seers with prostration, inquiry, and service; knowledge as the raft that carries even the worst sinner, the fire that burns karma, the purifier found in oneself by time — then, doubt slain by the sword of knowledge, arise.

verses.devanagari:
अपि चेदसि पापेभ्यः सर्वेभ्यः पापकृत्तमः ।
सर्वं ज्ञानप्लवेनैव वृजिनं सन्तरिष्यसि॥
verses.transliteration:
api cedasi pāpebhyaḥ sarvebhyaḥ pāpakṛttamaḥ
sarvaṃ jñānaplavenaiva vṛjinaṃ santariṣyasi
verses.essence: Even if you were the most sinful of all sinners, by the raft of knowledge alone you would cross over all wickedness.
verses.translation: Even if thou art the most sinful of all sinners, yet thou shalt verily cross all sins by the raft of knowledge.

anvaya.meaning: even if you were, than (all) sinners
of all the most sinful
all (of it), by the raft of knowledge alone
wickedness you will cross over

verses.explanation: The amnesty clause, stated at the limit case: take the worst record imaginable — knowledge still carries it across. The image is exact: a raft does not dry up the river of past karma; it keeps the passenger above it during the crossing. Sin, in this vision, is not a stain requiring eternal payment but a weight requiring sufficient buoyancy.
verses.connection: Pre-empts Arjuna's lingering terror from 1.36–45: no accumulated guilt disqualifies the knower.
verses.reflection: Whatever your worst chapter, the raft of knowing still floats — step on, today, from where you are.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the way to that knowledge and a call to arms: approach the seers with prostration, inquiry, and service; knowledge as the raft that carries even the worst sinner, the fire that burns karma, the purifier found in oneself by time — then, doubt slain by the sword of knowledge, arise.

verses.devanagari:
यथैधांसि समिद्धोऽग्निर्भस्मसात्कुरुतेऽर्जुन ।
ज्ञानाग्निः सर्वकर्माणि भस्मसात्कुरुते तथा॥
verses.transliteration:
yathaidhāṃsi samiddho'gnirbhasmasātkurute'rjuna
jñānāgniḥ sarvakarmāṇi bhasmasātkurute tathā
verses.essence: As a kindled fire reduces firewood to ash, Arjuna, so the fire of knowledge reduces all karma to ash.
verses.translation: As the blazing fire reduces fuel to ashes, O Arjuna, so does the fire of knowledge reduce all actions to ashes.

anvaya.meaning: as firewood, a well-kindled
fire reduces to ashes
the fire of knowledge, all karmas
reduces to ashes likewise

verses.explanation: The raft becomes a furnace. Logs do not negotiate with fire, do not get partially excused — they become ash, a substance that can never be wood again. So with karma met by realized knowledge: not suspended, not forgiven on probation, but converted past recall. Bhasmasāt — 'to the state of ash' — is the strongest word the language has for irreversibility.
verses.connection: Intensifies 4.36: crossing over becomes burning up — the residue itself is destroyed.
verses.reflection: Feed one old karma to the fire of understanding today: see it fully, and watch it turn to ash.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the way to that knowledge and a call to arms: approach the seers with prostration, inquiry, and service; knowledge as the raft that carries even the worst sinner, the fire that burns karma, the purifier found in oneself by time — then, doubt slain by the sword of knowledge, arise.

verses.devanagari:
न हि ज्ञानेन सदृशं पवित्रमिह विद्यते ।
तत्स्वयं योगसंसिद्धः कालेनात्मनि विन्दति॥
verses.transliteration:
na hi jñānena sadṛśaṃ pavitramiha vidyate
tatsvayaṃ yogasaṃsiddhaḥ kālenātmani vindati
verses.essence: Nothing here purifies like knowledge. One perfected in yoga finds it, in time, within oneself.
verses.translation: Verily, there is no purifier in this world like knowledge. He who is perfected in Yoga finds it in the Self in time.

anvaya.meaning: for nothing equal to knowledge
purifying exists here
that, oneself, one perfected in yoga
in time, finds within the self

verses.explanation: The superlative and the timetable. No purifier matches knowledge — not pilgrimage, fire, or austerity, each of which cleans a layer while knowledge dissolves the misidentification beneath all layers. And the finding is svayam... ātmani: within oneself, ripened by practice and kālena, by time. The teacher of 4.34 points; the treasure was always under the student's own floor. Na hi jnanena sadrisham — nothing purifies like knowledge: the chapter's superlative. The tradition pairs it with the verse's quiet timeline — kalena atmani vindati, one finds it in oneself, in time — reading patience into the promise. Yoga-samsiddhah: the practice ripens the vessel; the knowing then arrives like a season, not a seizure.
verses.connection: Crowns the fire-and-raft verses and relocates the prize: knowledge is found, not imported.
verses.reflection: Trust the timeline: purified by practice, clarity finds you by itself, in time, within.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the way to that knowledge and a call to arms: approach the seers with prostration, inquiry, and service; knowledge as the raft that carries even the worst sinner, the fire that burns karma, the purifier found in oneself by time — then, doubt slain by the sword of knowledge, arise.

verses.devanagari:
श्रद्धावाँल्लभते ज्ञानं तत्परः संयतेन्द्रियः ।
ज्ञानं लब्ध्वा परां शान्तिमचिरेणाधिगच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
śraddhāvā.Nllabhate jñānaṃ tatparaḥ saṃyatendriyaḥ
jñānaṃ labdhvā parāṃ śāntimacireṇādhigacchati
verses.essence: The one of faith, devoted to it, senses mastered, attains knowledge; having attained knowledge, one quickly reaches the supreme peace.
verses.translation: The man who is full of faith, who is devoted to it, and who has subdued the senses obtains (this) knowledge; and having obtained the knowledge he attains at once to the supreme peace.

anvaya.meaning: the one of faith attains knowledge
devoted to it, of restrained senses
having attained knowledge, the supreme peace
before long, one reaches

verses.explanation: The recipe in three ingredients: śraddhā, trust enough to commit before proof; tatparatā, making it the chief pursuit rather than a hobby; saṁyatendriyatā, senses gathered so the inquiry has fuel. Then the timeline shifts: acireṇa, *quickly*. The path is long only while one walks it half-turned-around. Shraddhavan labhate jnanam — the faithful one attains knowledge: the verse that settles faith's role in an inquiry tradition. Shraddha here is not credulity but working trust — the provisional confidence that lets practice run long enough to verify. The sequence is complete in one line: faith, dedication, restraint, knowledge, and then — achirena, without delay — the supreme peace.
verses.connection: Names who actually finds what 4.38 promised — and the speed available to the wholehearted.
verses.reflection: Bring faith, devotion, and a governed tongue to your study today — the three keys that open texts.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the way to that knowledge and a call to arms: approach the seers with prostration, inquiry, and service; knowledge as the raft that carries even the worst sinner, the fire that burns karma, the purifier found in oneself by time — then, doubt slain by the sword of knowledge, arise.

verses.devanagari:
अज्ञश्चाश्रद्दधानश्च संशयात्मा विनश्यति ।
नायं लोकोऽस्ति न परो न सुखं संशयात्मनः॥
verses.transliteration:
ajñaścāśraddadhānaśca saṃśayātmā vinaśyati
nāyaṃ loko'sti na paro na sukhaṃ saṃśayātmanaḥ
verses.essence: The ignorant, the faithless and the doubting self perish. For the doubter there is neither this world nor the next, nor happiness.
verses.translation: The ignorant the faithless, the doubting self goes to destruction; there is neither this world nor the other, nor happiness for the doubting.

anvaya.meaning: the ignorant and the faithless
and the doubting self perishes

anvaya.word: न अयं लोकः अस्ति न परः
anvaya.meaning: neither this world is, nor the next
nor happiness, for the doubting self

verses.explanation: Three failure modes, and the verse reserves its full sentence for the third: not honest ignorance, not even unbelief, but saṁśayātmā — the one whose very self has become doubt, the chronic suspender who can neither commit to the world nor to the path. Such a one loses both shores at once. Inquiry is sacred (4.34 commanded it); indefinite hovering is its parasite.
verses.connection: The shadow of 4.39's śraddhā: trust finds quickly; institutionalized doubt finds nothing anywhere.
verses.reflection: Notice doubt's cost honestly: neither this world enjoyed nor the next pursued. Choose a foothold.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the way to that knowledge and a call to arms: approach the seers with prostration, inquiry, and service; knowledge as the raft that carries even the worst sinner, the fire that burns karma, the purifier found in oneself by time — then, doubt slain by the sword of knowledge, arise.

verses.devanagari:
योगसंन्यस्तकर्माणं ज्ञानसञ्छिन्नसंशयम् ।
आत्मवन्तं न कर्माणि निबध्नन्ति धनञ्जय॥
verses.transliteration:
yogasaṃnyastakarmāṇaṃ jñānasañchinnasaṃśayam
ātmavantaṃ na karmāṇi nibadhnanti dhanañjaya
verses.essence: One whose actions are renounced in yoga, whose doubt is severed by knowledge, who is self-possessed — actions do not bind that one, Dhananjaya.
verses.translation: He who has renounced actions by Yoga, whose doubts are rent asunder by knowledge, and who is self-possessed actions do not bind him, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: one whose actions are renounced through yoga
whose doubt is cut asunder by knowledge
the self-possessed, not actions
bind, O Dhananjaya

verses.explanation: The chapter gathered into one figure: works laid down *through* yoga (not abandoned but offered — the whole point of 4.20–24), doubt not argued away but *cut* by direct knowledge, and ātmavantam, standing in oneself. Against such a person, karma has lost every handle it ever held.
verses.connection: Synthesis of the chapter's three currents — yajna, jnana, and the severing of 4.40's doubt — in a single freed worker.
verses.reflection: Renounce one fruit consciously, slice one doubt with knowledge, and feel how lightly the day binds.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the way to that knowledge and a call to arms: approach the seers with prostration, inquiry, and service; knowledge as the raft that carries even the worst sinner, the fire that burns karma, the purifier found in oneself by time — then, doubt slain by the sword of knowledge, arise.

verses.devanagari:
तस्मादज्ञानसम्भूतं हृत्स्थं ज्ञानासिनात्मनः ।
छित्त्वैनं संशयं योगमातिष्ठोत्तिष्ठ भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
tasmādajñānasambhūtaṃ hṛtsthaṃ jñānāsinātmanaḥ
chittvainaṃ saṃśayaṃ yogamātiṣṭhottiṣṭha bhārata
verses.essence: Therefore, with the sword of knowledge cut this doubt born of ignorance lodged in your heart. Take refuge in yoga — and stand up, Bharata!
verses.translation: Therefore with the sword of the knowledge (of the Self) cut asunder the doubt of the self born of ignorance, residing in thy heart, and take refuge in Yoga. Arise, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: therefore, this born of ignorance
seated in the heart, with the sword of knowledge, your own
having cut this doubt
take your stand in yoga — arise, O Bharata

verses.explanation: The chapter ends with steel: jñānāsi, knowledge as sword — fitting tribute to a warrior who will not be healed by anything he cannot wield. The doubt to be cut is hṛt-stham, lodged in the heart, not the head; arguments alone never reach it. Two final imperatives ring like commands on a field: ātiṣṭha, take your stand; uttiṣṭha, rise. The same word that failed in 2.3 returns — but now the man being raised has been given everything he needs to stand.
verses.connection: Closes the chapter by arming Arjuna against 4.40's enemy — and repeating, with new foundations, the call he once refused.
verses.reflection: Take the sword to your oldest hesitation now — then stand up into the duty it was blocking.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the way to that knowledge and a call to arms: approach the seers with prostration, inquiry, and service; knowledge as the raft that carries even the worst sinner, the fire that burns karma, the purifier found in oneself by time — then, doubt slain by the sword of knowledge, arise.
